description: This set of figures demonstrates the microstructures of two typical Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels. Subfigures (a) and (b) are optical microscopy (OM) images: (a) shows the annealed fully austenitic steel with a 50 μm scale bar, consisting of equiaxed austenite grains with abundant annealing twins and fully recovered grain structure; (b) presents the annealed austenite-based duplex steel with a 20 μm scale bar, where elongated ferrite grains are distributed along the rolling direction (marked with rolling direction RD and normal direction ND) in the austenite matrix. Subfigures (c) and (d) are corresponding schematic diagrams for clearer morphological demonstration: (c) labels austenite grains and annealing twins for single-phase austenitic steel, (d) labels austenite matrix and ferrite grains for duplex steel. The figure reveals the distinct microstructure difference between the two steel types, verifying that alloying and heat treatment can regulate the phase composition and microstructure of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels.

This set of three isothermal phase sections of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels are plotted at 900 °C, with subplot (a), (b), (c) corresponding to fixed Mn content of 10 wt%, 20 wt%, 30 wt% respectively. The horizontal axis represents Al content in wt% (range 0-14 wt%), and the vertical axis represents C content in wt% (range 0-4 wt%). Light gray, gold, and blue regions represent austenite (γ), ferrite (α), and κ-carbide phase respectively, with multiple two-phase and three-phase regions (including γ+M₃C, γ+κ, γ+M₃C+κ, γ+α, γ+κ+α, α+κ, B2+κ) labeled, and experimental validation points overlaid. Key trends revealed: (1) increasing Mn content expands the γ phase region at Al content >8 wt%, and expands the κ phase region at C content >3 wt% and Al content >10 wt%; (2) M₃C and κ phases precipitate when C content is higher than 1.2 wt%; (3) increasing Al content expands the α phase region in alloys with 20-30 wt% Mn. This phase diagram at 900 °C, which is close to industrial hot rolling temperature, provides critical guidance for alloy composition design and thermomechanical processing of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density structural steels.

This figure is a time-temperature transformation diagram illustrating γ (austenite) phase decomposition behavior of Fe–28Mn–8.5Al–1C–1.2Si low-density steel during cooling/aging treatment. The x-axis represents time (range: 0.01–1000 h), and the y-axis represents temperature (range: 300–1000 °C). Two cooling regimes are marked: fast cooling (retains single γ phase at low temperature, with phase region γ+κ' at intermediate temperature) and slow cooling (triggers sequential γ decomposition). A threshold cooling rate of -4.4 °C/h is marked as the boundary for notable γ decomposition. Identified phase regions include single γ phase, γ+κ', γ+κ*, γ+κ'+κ*, γ+κ*+α(B2/DO₃), and γ+κ+B2/DO₃, with ordered phase intervals (high-temperature B2, intermediate B2+DO₃, low-temperature DO₃) labelled on the right. The core conclusion is that cooling rates slower than 4.4 °C/h induce precipitation of κ carbides and other secondary phases from the γ matrix, so adjusting cooling rate can control phase evolution, microstructure and final mechanical properties of the low-density steel.

This figure contains 6 subfigures to correlate deformation twinning behavior with work hardening performance in Fe-Mn-C and Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels. Subfigures (a) and (b) are schematics illustrating dislocation-twin interactions: (a) shows dislocations pinned at primary twin boundaries, (b) demonstrates secondary twins further dividing austenite grains. Subfigures (c) (scale bar 500 nm) and (d) (scale bar 200 nm) are TEM bright-field images with selected area electron diffraction patterns, exhibiting morphologies of deformation twins and ε-twins, including cross twin structures. Subfigure (e) shows the ratio of twinned grain fraction (F) to twin thickness (T) versus true strain: FeMnC steel has F/T rising from ~0.4 to ~5.9 with strain, much higher than <0.7 for FeMnCAl steel. Subfigure (f) presents true stress-strain and work-hardening rate curves: 0Al (FeMnC) steel has higher work-hardening rate and strength than 1.5Al (FeMnCAl) steel. The core conclusion is that Al addition suppresses deformation twinning, reduces F/T ratio, and weakens the dynamic Hall-Petch effect from TWIP, thus lowering work hardening capability.

This figure consists of three subplots for mechanical performance analysis of constituent phases in Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel. Subplot (a) plots stress and work hardening rate of ferrite and bainite as a function of effective plastic strain, with measured necking strains of 0.163 for ferrite and 0.127 for bainite. Subplot (b) shows the corresponding stress and work hardening rate curves for austenite and martensite, with necking strains of 0.348 for austenite and 0.50 for martensite, demonstrating that martensite possesses the highest work hardening rate among all four phases. Subplot (c) is a schematic of the Considere criterion, illustrating that the necking point occurs at the intersection of the work hardening rate curve and true stress-strain curve, and higher work hardening rate contributes to higher uniform elongation and tensile strength. This figure supports the conclusion that the TRIP effect, which refers to retained austenite transforming to martensite during plastic deformation, improves mechanical properties of the low-density steel by introducing high-hardening martensite to delay necking.

This figure characterizes the correlation between Al content, dislocation slip behavior, microstructure, and tensile mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is a line plot showing W_R, a parameter related to dislocation slip mode, decreases linearly from ~29×10⁻⁴ m to ~10×10⁻⁴ m as Al content rises from 0 to 6 wt.%, indicating a slip mode transition. Subfigure (a1) is a TEM micrograph (200 nm scale bar) of 3 wt.% Al alloy at true strain 0.1, showing dislocation cells (DCs) corresponding to wavy slip, while subfigure (a2) (TEM, 200 nm scale bar, 6 wt.% Al alloy, true strain 0.1) exhibits clear planar slip features. Subfigure (b) is an ECCI micrograph (1 μm scale bar) showing microband structure at true strain ε=0.14. Subfigure (c) displays true stress-strain curve (solid line, tested until fracture) and strain hardening rate curve (dotted line), where the hardening rate first drops rapidly then remains at a high plateau before gradual reduction. The figure supports that increasing Al addition promotes planar dislocation slip, and short range order induced microband formation contributes to the superior work hardening capacity of the low-density steel.

This 3D chart illustrates the calculated relationship between yield strength improvement (Δσₚ, unit: MPa, z-axis), carbide size (unit: nm, x-axis), and carbide volume fraction f (unit: %, y-axis) in Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel. Experimentally reported data points for different carbide types, including TiC, V(C,N), V₄C₃, VC/Fe₃C, VC, and NbC, are plotted on the calculated surface, with corresponding literature citation numbers marked in brackets. The trend shows that higher volume fraction and smaller size of carbide precipitates lead to more significant yield strength improvement, with the maximum Δσₚ approaching 900 MPa. This figure supports the core conclusion that carbide precipitates act as effective obstacles to dislocation slip during plastic deformation, delivering notable precipitation strengthening effect to enhance the mechanical performance of Fe–Mn–C–Al steel.

This figure consists of 5 subfigures investigating the wear performance of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels under different aging conditions. Subfigure (a) plots the wear rate (unit: ×10³ mm³/Nm) of Fe–22.33Mn–1.35C–9.87Al steel as a function of aging time (0-3 h) at 500℃, 550℃, 600℃, all showing linear decreasing trends with fitting R² of 0.97, 0.93, 0.99 respectively, and the as-homogenized sample has a wear rate of ~0.36×10³ mm³/Nm. Subfigures (b) and (c) are SEM images of worn surface (with observed craters) and wear debris of the steel aged at 600℃ for 3h, verifying its good wear resistance. Subfigures (d) and (e) are SEM images of worn surfaces of Fe–28Mn–1C–8.5Al steel quenched at 1050℃ then aged at 550℃ for 3h and 4h respectively: overaging (4h) induces delamination, broken coarse (Fe, Mn)₃AlC κ-carbides and microcracking, deteriorating wear resistance. The core conclusion is that aging parameters (temperature, time) significantly regulate the wear property of Fe-Mn-C-Al steels via tailoring κ-carbide precipitation, and overaging leads to degraded wear performance.

This group of optical micrographs presents the microstructure of Fe–22Mn–0.4C–1.5Al–1.5Si (wt%) low-density steel fabricated via metal injection molding (MIM), sintered at 1360 °C for different durations. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the sample sintered for 1 h, and subfigure (b) corresponds to the sample sintered for 3 h, both with a 200 μm scale bar. Two characteristic constituents are clearly identified: equiaxed austenitic matrix grains, and dark manganese oxide particles distributed mainly along grain boundaries and partially inside grains. Comparative observation shows that extending the sintering duration from 1 h to 3 h leads to obvious growth of austenitic grains, while manganese oxide particles maintain uniform distribution. The figure verifies that MIM followed by high-temperature sintering can produce fully austenitic low-density steel with evenly dispersed manganese oxide inclusions, which is expected to improve the wear resistance of this type of structural steel.

This figure is a fabrication roadmap schematic for Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel. It presents two core sequential processing stages: first, alloying design, which regulates steel density, precipitation behavior, and strengthening mechanisms of the alloy; second, annealing and aging treatments, which induce phase transformation and precipitate growth to modulate the microstructure. The final obtained low-density steel has four key advantages: lower density, higher strength, good wear resistance, and low material cost. This roadmap clarifies the process-performance correlation of low-density steel, supporting the optimization of preparation procedures to promote the engineering application of this lightweight structural material, as noted that mass production also requires solving challenges of low heat dissipation rate of high-Mn ingots and Al-oxide contamination during casting and rolling.
